skill.name: terraform-skill
skill.description: Guidance for designing, testing, securing, and operating Terraform and OpenTofu infrastructure code.
skill.summary: Apply Terraform and OpenTofu best practices for modules, tests, CI/CD, security, and version management.
skill.author: Anton Babenko

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, sensitive-file, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and Terraform terminology rather than executable code. The skill contains no evidence of prompt injection, hidden data exfiltration, or malicious intent. Publication is reasonable with a low-risk note because users may ask an agent to run Terraform and security scanning commands from the guidance.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Commands Require User Review
low_findings.description: The skill recommends Terraform, OpenTofu, Trivy, and Checkov commands for validation, scanning, initialization, testing, and planning. These are legitimate DevOps commands, but an agent should not run infrastructure-changing commands without explicit user approval and environment review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain visible Markdown examples and command names, not hidden execution logic. The risk is operational misuse, not malicious code execution.

low_findings.title: Network and CIDR Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is source metadata, and the IP-like values are private Terraform CIDR examples. The guidance also warns against public security group exposure, so these patterns do not indicate network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review confirms the URL identifies the upstream skill source and the IP values are RFC1918 CIDR examples in Terraform snippets. No outbound request code or data transfer behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Sensitive-State and Certificate Terms Are Educational
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged state and key-related terms, but the reviewed context discusses securing secrets, sensitive outputs, and state file hygiene. No evidence found that the skill reads, writes, or exfiltrates Terraform state, certificates, or private keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear in best-practice guidance and command documentation. There is no executable code path or instruction to collect secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Treated as Ruby Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer interpreted Markdown backticks and fenced code blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is a skill document with HCL, Bash, tables, and inline command names, not Ruby code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows Markdown fences and Terraform examples rather than Ruby syntax. This strongly supports dismissing the execution heuristic.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords in Terraform Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak-cryptography alerts match words inside Terraform documentation and version examples, including module names, function names, and prose. No cryptographic implementation or recommendation to use weak algorithms was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static labels do not align with the visible context, which is Terraform structure and feature guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak algorithm use.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Infrastructure Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: System reconnaissance alerts correspond to infrastructure testing, dependency, and CIDR examples. These are expected in Terraform guidance and do not instruct unauthorized host discovery or credential harvesting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are diagrams, HCL examples, and version guidance. They lack commands or logic for probing systems outside the user infrastructure workflow.

content.value_statement: Terraform projects can become hard to test, review, and operate across teams. This skill provides structured guidance for modules, testing, CI/CD, security, and version management.
actual_capabilities: Recommends Terraform and OpenTofu project structures for environments, modules, examples, and tests.
Explains when to use static validation, native Terraform tests, Terratest, mocking, and policy checks.
Provides naming, file organization, variable, output, count, and for_each guidance for reusable modules.
Outlines CI/CD stages for validation, testing, planning, approval, and cost-aware integration testing.
Summarizes security practices such as encryption, least privilege, secret handling, and state file hygiene.
Covers version constraint strategies and modern Terraform features from Terraform 1.0 through 1.11.
limitations: It does not replace provider-specific API documentation or live provider schemas.
It does not verify cloud account permissions, costs, quotas, or organization policies.
It may need updates when Terraform, OpenTofu, or provider behavior changes.
It provides guidance and examples, but users must review plans before applying infrastructure changes.

use_cases.title: Design a Reusable Terraform Module
use_cases.description: Plan module structure, inputs, outputs, examples, and tests before publishing shared infrastructure code.

use_cases.title: Improve IaC Pull Request Checks
use_cases.description: Choose validation, linting, security scanning, and test stages for a Terraform or OpenTofu CI pipeline.

use_cases.title: Review Infrastructure Code Quality
use_cases.description: Evaluate naming, resource layout, version constraints, state handling, and security practices in an existing project.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Module Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Terraform skill to propose a clean module layout for a small AWS networking module. Include files, variables, outputs, examples, and basic tests.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Testing Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Terraform skill to recommend a testing strategy for this Terraform module. Compare validate, tflint, security scanning, native tests, and Terratest.

prompt_templates.title: Review a CI/CD Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Terraform skill to review this infrastructure CI workflow. Identify missing validation, plan review, security scanning, approvals, and cleanup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Production Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Terraform skill to assess this production module for naming, for_each usage, state safety, dependency ordering, version constraints, and migration risk.

output_examples.input: Review my Terraform module structure for maintainability.
output: A concise assessment of module boundaries and file organization.
Specific changes for variables, outputs, examples, and tests.
Risk notes for state, naming, and breaking changes.

output_examples.input: Help me choose tests for a new OpenTofu module.
output: A testing recommendation based on Terraform or OpenTofu version and module complexity.
Suggested static checks, native tests, and integration tests.
Cost and cleanup considerations for real infrastructure tests.

output_examples.input: Improve my Terraform CI pipeline before production use.
output: A staged workflow covering format, validation, linting, security scanning, testing, plan review, and apply approval.
Notes on when to mock providers and when to run real integration tests.
Operational safeguards for cost control and resource cleanup.

best_practices: Ask for the Terraform or OpenTofu version, provider, module purpose, and deployment environment before generating recommendations.
Review every plan and command before applying infrastructure changes, especially in shared or production accounts.
Use provider documentation and schemas when exact resource attributes or block types matter.
anti_patterns: Do not treat examples as production-ready without adapting naming, permissions, encryption, and state configuration.
Do not run apply, destroy, or upgrade commands without explicit approval and a reviewed plan.
Do not use the skill as the only source for provider-specific API behavior or compliance requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill work for both Terraform and OpenTofu?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers both tools and highlights version-specific guidance where behavior or features differ.

faq.question: Can it generate complete Terraform modules?
faq.answer: It can guide module structure and generate examples, but users must validate provider schemas and review plans.

faq.question: Does it run Terraform commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents commands and workflows. Any command execution depends on the user and the host AI tool.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production infrastructure?
faq.answer: It is useful for production planning and review, but final changes need human approval, testing, and policy checks.

faq.question: Does it replace provider documentation?
faq.answer: No. Use provider documentation for exact resource arguments, computed values, and API-specific behavior.

faq.question: What security topics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers secret handling, encryption, least privilege, static scanning, compliance checks, and Terraform state safety.
